perturbation.original_text: During the nine (9) month period commencing on the Effective Date, neither Party nor any of such Party’s Representatives will directly or indirectly solicit for employment, hire or induce or encourage (in each case, other than by means of a general solicitation pursuant to a newspaper or other media advertisement or other customary means by such party in the ordinary course of its business) any Covered Person (as defined herein) to terminate his or her relationship with the other Party or any subsidiary or other affiliate of said other Party in order to become an employee, consultant or independent contractor of or to any other Person.
perturbation.changed_text: During the three (3) month period commencing on the Effective Date, neither Party nor any of such Party’s Representatives will directly or indirectly solicit for employment, hire or induce or encourage (in each case, other than by means of a general solicitation pursuant to a newspaper or other media advertisement or other customary means by such party in the ordinary course of its business) any Covered Person (as defined herein) to terminate his or her relationship with the other Party or any subsidiary or other affiliate of said other Party in order to become an employee, consultant or independent contractor of or to any other Person.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-solicitation period to 3 months may contradict state laws regarding the enforceability of non-solicitation agreements, especially if such agreements are deemed unreasonably short.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires that non-compete/non-solicitation agreements are reasonable in scope and duration. A three-month non-solicitation period might be deemed unreasonably short depending on the specific industry, job function, and competitive landscape, making it unenforceable. Delaware courts assess the reasonableness of such restrictions based on the specific facts of each case.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the non-solicitation period from nine months to three months. The Law Explanation states that Delaware law requires non-compete/non-solicitation agreements to be reasonable in scope and duration, and a three-month period might be deemed unreasonably short, making it unenforceable. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address non-solicitation agreements, the Law Explanation's assertion, combined with the Perturbation Explanation that highlights potential state law contradictions, suggests a sufficiently strong legal contradiction in the context of the changed duration. Therefore, because the modified duration may contradict state law, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: During the one-year period commencing on the Effective Date (the “ Standstill Period ”), each Party (hereinafter a “Restricted Party”) agrees that it will not, in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) make, effect, initiate, propose, cause, participate in or knowingly encourage (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of the other Party (“Other Party”) or any securities of any subsidiary of the Other Party, (ii) any acquisition of any material assets of the Other Party or the Other Party’s subsidiaries outside the ordinary course of business, (iii) any tender offer, exchange offer, merger, business combination, recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or extraordinary transaction involving the Other Party or the Other Party’s subsidiaries, or involving any securities or material assets of the Other Party or the Other Party’s subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as those terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents with respect to any securities of the Other Party; (b) form or join a “group” (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the rules promulgated thereunder) with respect to the beneficial ownership of any securities of the Other Party; (c) act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control the management, board of directors or policies of the Other Party; (d) take any action that would reasonably be likely to require the Other Party to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in clause “(a)” of this sentence; or (e) enter into any arrangement or agreement with any other Person relating to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: During the five-year period commencing on the Effective Date (the “ Standstill Period ”), each Party (hereinafter a “Restricted Party”) agrees that it will not, in any manner, directly or indirectly: (a) make, effect, initiate, propose, cause, participate in or knowingly encourage (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of the other Party (“Other Party”) or any securities of any subsidiary of the Other Party, (ii) any acquisition of any material assets of the Other Party or the Other Party’s subsidiaries outside the ordinary course of business, (iii) any tender offer, exchange offer, merger, business combination, recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or extraordinary transaction involving the Other Party or the Other Party’s subsidiaries, or involving any securities or material assets of the Other Party or the Other Party’s subsidiaries, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as those terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents with respect to any securities of the Other Party; (b) form or join a “group” (as defined in the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and the rules promulgated thereunder) with respect to the beneficial ownership of any securities of the Other Party; (c) act, alone or in concert with others, to seek to control the management, board of directors or policies of the Other Party; (d) take any action that would reasonably be likely to require the Other Party to make a public announcement regarding any of the types of matters set forth in clause “(a)” of this sentence; or (e) enter into any arrangement or agreement with any other Person relating to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the standstill period to five years might be considered unreasonable under Delaware law, potentially violating fiduciary duties of directors if it unduly restricts their ability to consider future opportunities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law regarding fiduciary duties
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law mandates that corporate directors act in the best interests of the corporation and its shareholders. A five-year standstill provision may be viewed as an unreasonable restriction on a director's ability to fulfill their fiduciary duties if it prevents them from exploring beneficial transactions or opportunities for the company after the initial year.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the standstill period from one year to five years. The perturbation explanation and law explanation suggest this may conflict with Delaware law, as it could unreasonably restrict directors' fiduciary duties to explore beneficial transactions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, particularly section (a), states that 'the business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors'. A five-year standstill period may limit the board's ability to manage the corporation effectively if opportunities arise during that period, which contradicts their duty to act in the best interests of the corporation, as stated in the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise set forth herein, this Agreement shall terminate on the second anniversary of the Effective Date; provided, however, that (i) the restrictions and covenants set forth in this Agreement relating to Confidential Information consisting of or relating to trade secrets, intellectual property, intellectual property rights and/or technical information shall terminate on the fifth anniversary of the Effective Date; and (ii) the termination of this Agreement shall not relieve any Party from any liability with respect to any violation or breach of any provision contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise set forth herein, this Agreement shall terminate on the first anniversary of the Effective Date; provided, however, that (i) the restrictions and covenants set forth in this Agreement relating to Confidential Information consisting of or relating to trade secrets, intellectual property, intellectual property rights and/or technical information shall terminate on the second anniversary of the Effective Date; and (ii) the termination of this Agreement shall not relieve any Party from any liability with respect to any violation or breach of any provision contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Shortening the protection period for trade secrets to 2 years may be insufficient under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), especially if that duration doesn't allow the trade secret holder to realize the value of the trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. By limiting the confidentiality obligations to only two years, the agreement could fail to adequately protect trade secrets that would normally be expected to remain confidential and valuable for a longer period. The DTSA provides remedies for misappropriation as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret; setting an arbitrary limit could render the trade secret unprotected even if it is still valuable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the protection period for trade secrets from five years to two years. The Law Explanation states that the DTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. By limiting the confidentiality obligations to two years, the agreement could fail to adequately protect trade secrets that would normally be expected to remain confidential and valuable for a longer period. The scraped law snippet 2, 18 USC 1836, pertains to civil proceedings for misappropriation of trade secrets, indicating that the law intends to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. Therefore, limiting the protection to a shorter time frame could contradict the intent of the law to protect trade secrets as long as they remain valuable.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the other Party or any of the other Party’s Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will be entitled to seek monetary damages only, as a remedy for any breach or threatened breach of this Agreement by the other Party or any of the other Party’s Representatives. Injunctive relief and specific performance are explicitly waived.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the possibility of injunctive relief for breaches of confidentiality may violate the DTSA by preventing adequate remedies for trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA explicitly allows for injunctive relief to prevent trade secret misappropriation. By waiving the right to seek injunctive relief, the contract limits the remedies available to a party whose trade secrets have been misappropriated, which undermines the protection afforded by the DTSA. While parties can contractually limit remedies, a complete waiver of equitable relief may be deemed unconscionable if it leaves a party with no adequate means to stop ongoing or threatened misappropriation.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the spirit and potential application of the DTSA (as interpreted by the LLM Law Explanation), which allows for injunctive relief to prevent trade secret misappropriation. The original contract allows for equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance. The changed contract explicitly waives injunctive relief. While the provided snippets do not contain the explicit text of the DTSA, Snippet 2 (18 USC 1836) discusses civil proceedings related to trade secrets and refers to injunctive relief. The removal of the possibility of injunctive relief significantly weakens the protection against trade secret misappropriation that the DTSA intends to provide, creating a legal contradiction. The law explanation explicitly stated that a complete waiver of equitable relief may be deemed unconscionable if it leaves a party with no adequate means to stop ongoing or threatened misappropriation.
